citation: Currier BS et al. American College of Sports Medicine Position Stand: Resistance Training Prescription for Muscle Function, Hypertrophy, and Physical Performance in Healthy Adults. Med Sci Sports Exerc. 2026.
key_finding: Progressive resistance training improves strength, hypertrophy and function. Higher weekly volume tends to enhance hypertrophy; consistency and progression matter more than many minor programming details.
application: Three full-body sessions, progressive overload, 2-4 sets, mostly 1-3 RIR, gradual volume progression.
limitations: Healthy-adult evidence; not a scoliosis-specific prescription.

citation: Monticone M et al. Adults with idiopathic scoliosis improve disability after motor and cognitive rehabilitation: a randomized controlled trial. Eur Spine J. 2016.
key_finding: Active self-correction, task-oriented exercise and cognitive-behavioural rehabilitation outperformed general physiotherapy for disability; benefits persisted at one year.
application: Use active self-correction and task integration only after curve-specific instruction; include stress/cognitive load management.
limitations: Main curve <35 degrees; mean age about 52; one programme cannot define all adult scoliosis care.

citation: Alanazi MH et al. Effect of stabilization exercise on back pain, disability and quality of life in adults with scoliosis: a systematic review. Eur J Phys Rehabil Med. 2018.
key_finding: Only one eligible controlled study was found; adult scoliosis exercise evidence remains sparse.
application: Do not overclaim structural correction. Treat generic core work as capacity training, not curve correction.
limitations: Extremely limited evidence base.

citation: Negrini S et al. 2016 SOSORT guidelines: orthopaedic and rehabilitation treatment of idiopathic scoliosis during growth. Scoliosis Spinal Disord. 2018.
key_finding: PSSE principles include 3D auto-correction, training activities of daily living, stabilizing the corrected posture and education.
application: The app locks side-specific correction until current curve data and clinician-taught instructions exist.
limitations: Primarily focused on scoliosis during growth, not a complete adult guideline.

citation: Berdishevsky H et al. Physiotherapy scoliosis-specific exercises – a comprehensive review of seven major schools. Scoliosis Spinal Disord. 2016.
key_finding: PSSE schools share 3D self-correction, ADL integration and stabilization, but methods and curve-pattern execution differ.
application: Generic symmetrical training is separated from clinician-prescribed PSSE.
limitations: Not proof that every method or exercise works equally; much evidence is adolescent-focused.

citation: Weinstein SL, Dolan LA, Cheng JCY, Danielsson AJ, Morcuende JA. Adolescent idiopathic scoliosis. Lancet. 2008;371(9623):1527-1537.
key_finding: AIS is a structural 3D spinal deformity; curve-progression risk rises with magnitude and skeletal immaturity. Management is observation, bracing, and surgery; loaded exercise is addressed descriptively, not as a progression trial.
application: Context source for the precautionary block on heavy axial loading in diagnosed moderate-or-greater / rigid / braced curves. Supports conservative caution only — not direct proof that load worsens curves.
limitations: Adolescent-focused overview (adult scoliosis extrapolated cautiously). Provides NO direct evidence that heavy axial loading worsens scoliosis; the contraindication is consensus/conservative and rated honestly as context-grade.

citation: Aragon AA et al. International Society of Sports Nutrition position stand: diets and body composition. J Int Soc Sports Nutr. 2017.
key_finding: Sustained energy deficit drives fat loss; slower loss better preserves lean mass in leaner trained people; higher protein may aid retention during a deficit.
application: Mild deficit, high protein, adaptive adjustment from actual weight trend and adherence.
limitations: Protein needs vary; the upper 2.3-3.1 g/kg FFM range is not a mandatory target for everyone.

citation: Morton RW et al. Protein supplementation and resistance training adaptations: systematic review, meta-analysis and meta-regression. Br J Sports Med. 2018.
key_finding: Protein supports resistance-training gains; average benefit plateau was around 1.6 g/kg/day with uncertainty extending higher.
application: Default 1.8-2.2 g/kg/day during a cut; 170 g/day here is about 2.0 g/kg.
limitations: Supplement studies; individual response, total energy and baseline intake matter.

citation: Mifflin MD et al. A new predictive equation for resting energy expenditure in healthy individuals. Am J Clin Nutr. 1990.
key_finding: Provides a practical RMR estimate from weight, height, age and sex.
application: One of two starting BMR estimates; maintenance is calibrated from observed trend.
limitations: Prediction error at individual level; not a metabolic measurement.

citation: Cunningham JJ. Body composition as a determinant of energy expenditure: a synthetic review and a proposed general prediction equation. Am J Clin Nutr. 1991.
key_finding: Resting energy can be estimated from fat-free mass using 370 + 21.6 × FFM kg.
application: Shown as a secondary estimate only because FFM comes from imprecise consumer BIA.
limitations: Accuracy is constrained by body-composition measurement error.

citation: Frija-Masson J et al. Accuracy of Smart Scales on Weight and Body Composition: Observational Study. JMIR Mhealth Uhealth. 2021.
key_finding: Consumer smart scales measured body weight reasonably well but body-composition estimates were not accurate enough to replace DXA.
application: Use Xiaomi weight trends; treat body-fat, muscle, water and visceral-fat fields as noisy secondary signals.
limitations: Three tested devices, not every scale; study population was clinical.

citation: Lopez P et al. Resistance Training Load Effects on Muscle Hypertrophy and Strength Gain: Systematic Review and Network Meta-analysis. Med Sci Sports Exerc. 2021.
key_finding: Hypertrophy can occur across a wide loading range when sets are sufficiently effortful; heavier loads are superior for maximal strength.
application: With 10 kg dumbbells, use unilateral leverage, higher reps, pauses and 1-3 RIR.
limitations: Not scoliosis-specific; effort and technique control are critical with low loads.

citation: Kikuchi N, Nakazato K. Low-load bench press and push-up induce similar muscle hypertrophy and strength gain. J Exerc Sci Fit. 2017.
key_finding: Load-matched push-ups produced comparable hypertrophy and strength to low-load bench press.
application: Progressive push-up variations are a legitimate primary press exercise.
limitations: Small study and one movement pattern.

citation: Robinson ZP et al. Exploring the Dose-Response Relationship Between Estimated Resistance Training Proximity to Failure, Strength Gain, and Muscle Hypertrophy. Sports Med. 2024.
key_finding: Hypertrophy tends to improve closer to failure, while strength does not require constant failure training.
application: Most working sets at 1-3 RIR; avoid grinding failure on balance-demanding or spine-loaded patterns.
limitations: RIR estimation is noisy and causality/dose shape is uncertain.

citation: Ramos-Campo DJ et al. Efficacy of Split Versus Full-Body Resistance Training on Strength and Muscle Growth: A Systematic Review With Meta-Analysis. J Strength Cond Res. 2024.
key_finding: Full-body and split routines yield similar strength and hypertrophy when weekly volume is equated.
application: Three full-body sessions are a valid choice and fit the user's preference.
limitations: Programme quality and adherence matter more than the label.

citation: Bull FC et al. World Health Organization 2020 guidelines on physical activity and sedentary behaviour. Br J Sports Med. 2020.
key_finding: Adults should accumulate 150-300 minutes of moderate or 75-150 minutes of vigorous aerobic activity weekly plus strength work on at least 2 days.
application: Add brisk walking and break up desk time; strength sessions alone do not guarantee the aerobic target.
limitations: Population-level guideline, not a disease-specific prescription.

citation: Peddie MC et al. The effects of prolonged sitting, prolonged standing, and activity breaks on vascular function and postprandial metabolism. 2021.
key_finding: Neither prolonged sitting nor prolonged standing is ideal; regular movement breaks are preferable to simply replacing all sitting with standing.
application: Alternate positions and insert 2-3 minute movement breaks every 30-45 minutes.
limitations: Acute laboratory outcomes; exact break dose is not settled.

citation: Laborde S et al. Effects of voluntary slow breathing on heart rate and heart rate variability: systematic review and meta-analysis. Neurosci Biobehav Rev. 2022.
key_finding: Slow breathing acutely alters heart rate variability and autonomic markers.
application: Use 5-6 comfortable breaths/min for 5 minutes as stress regulation—not as structural scoliosis correction.
limitations: Heterogeneous protocols and outcomes; symptom relief is individual.

citation: Hopper SI et al. Effectiveness of diaphragmatic breathing for reducing physiological and psychological stress in adults: a quantitative systematic review. JBI Database System Rev Implement Rep. 2019.
key_finding: Diaphragmatic breathing may reduce physiological and self-reported stress.
application: Short daily practice, without forced maximal inhalation or prolonged breath holds.
limitations: Small heterogeneous studies and varying risk of bias.

citation: Wieland LS et al. Yoga for chronic non-specific low back pain. Cochrane Database Syst Rev. 2022.
key_finding: Yoga provides small improvements versus no exercise, but may increase minor adverse events such as transient back pain and is not clearly superior to other exercise.
application: Use gentle symptom-guided yoga as mobility/relaxation, not a curve-correction method.
limitations: Low-to-moderate certainty; not scoliosis-specific.

citation: Brinjikji W et al. Systematic literature review of imaging features of spinal degeneration in asymptomatic populations. AJNR. 2015.
key_finding: Disc degeneration and protrusions are common in asymptomatic people and become more prevalent with age.
application: Do not let mild MRI findings alone dictate fear-based movement avoidance; correlate with symptoms and examination.
limitations: Imaging findings can still be clinically relevant in context.

citation: American College of Radiology. ACR Appropriateness Criteria: Low Back Pain.
key_finding: Urgent evaluation/imaging is considered when red flags suggest cauda equina syndrome, malignancy, fracture or infection.
application: The app blocks self-programming when new bowel/bladder dysfunction, saddle anaesthesia, progressive weakness or other major red flags are reported.
limitations: A safety gate, not a diagnosis; red flags require clinical interpretation.
